content.cn_text: 请仔细听，并回答第31至40题。
content.en_text: I'll listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.

content.cn_text: 大家早上好。
content.en_text: Good morning everyone.

content.cn_text: 在今天的心理学讲座中，我将谈论人类记忆。
content.en_text: In today's psychology lecture, I'll be talking about human memory.

content.cn_text: 我首先想做的是建立历史背景。
content.en_text: And what I want to do first is establish the historical context.

content.cn_text: 所以我会概述关于人类记忆的各种观点，以及这些观点如何随时间发生变化。
content.en_text: So I'll outline various ideas about human memory and how they've changed over time.

content.cn_text: 首先，是早期文化。
content.en_text: So first, early cultures.

content.cn_text: 在最早期，比如公元前3000年以前，人们依赖于能够
content.en_text: Now, in the earliest times, let's say before 3000 years BC, people were dependent on being

content.cn_text: 记住事物，甚至可能比我们今天更加依赖记忆。
content.en_text: able to remember things, perhaps even more than we are today.

content.cn_text: 这是因为人类还不会书写，这种技能还没有发展出来。
content.en_text: That's because humans could not write, the skill hadn't been developed.

content.cn_text: 所以良好的记忆力很重要。
content.en_text: So a good memory was important.

content.cn_text: 一方面，它帮助人们在家周围的区域辨别方向，而且它是
content.en_text: For one thing, it helped people navigate in the area around their homes, and it was the

content.cn_text: 获取家族历史和祖先信息的唯一方式。
content.en_text: only way to access family history and ancestry.

content.cn_text: 此外，良好的记忆力可能关系到生死。
content.en_text: Once more, a good memory might be the difference between life and death.

content.cn_text: 大多数人是猎人和采集者，所以了解哪些食物
content.en_text: Most people were hunters and gatherers, so it was essential to understand which foods

content.cn_text: 有毒、哪些无毒是至关重要的。
content.en_text: were poisonous and which were not.

content.cn_text: 这一点对植物尤其如此，因为它们有时看起来非常相似，而
content.en_text: This was particularly true of plants because they sometimes looked very similar, and the

content.cn_text: 储存这些信息并传给下一代的唯一方式就是记住
content.en_text: only way to store this information and pass it on to the next generation was to remember

content.cn_text: 它。
content.en_text: it.

content.cn_text: 遗憾的是，由于那是很久以前的事，我们对当时使用了哪些技巧
content.en_text: Unfortunately, because it was so long ago, there's limited information about what techniques

content.cn_text: 来辅助记忆了解有限。
content.en_text: were used to assist memory.

content.cn_text: 好，现在我们来看古希腊。希腊诗人西摩尼得斯
content.en_text: Okay, so moving on now to the ancient Greeks, and the Greek poet named Simonides, who

content.cn_text: 生活在约公元前500年，有时被称为记忆训练之父。
content.en_text: lived around 500 BC, was the man sometimes described as the father of memory training.

content.cn_text: 西摩尼得斯最重要的观点是，如果我们把信息
content.en_text: Simonides' most important idea was that it's much easier to remember information if we

content.cn_text: 和某个特定的地点联系起来，就更容易记住。
content.en_text: connect it with one particular place.

content.cn_text: 随后几百年间，其他希腊学者发展了他的记忆理论，并且
content.en_text: Then, over hundreds of years, other Greek scholars developed his ideas about memory, and a

content.cn_text: 其中许多信息保存了下来。
content.en_text: lot of this information has survived.

content.cn_text: 所以我们知道，到一世纪时，希腊人认为人们可以利用他们的
content.en_text: So we know that, by the first century, the Greeks believed that people could use their

content.cn_text: 感官来提高记忆力，而这一点在涉及
content.en_text: human senses to improve their memory, and this was particularly true when it came to

content.cn_text: 视觉时尤其明显。
content.en_text: sight.

content.cn_text: 他们认为二者之间有很强的联系。
content.en_text: There was a strong connection there they believed.

content.cn_text: 接下来我应该谈谈古罗马人。他们和希腊人一样，认为记忆
content.en_text: Well, next I should talk about the ancient Romans, who, like the Greeks, believed memory

content.cn_text: 训练极其重要。
content.en_text: training was extremely important.

content.cn_text: 显然，他们以许多不同方式使用记忆，但最重要的是，在古罗马，人们
content.en_text: They used memory in many different ways, obviously, but most of all, in ancient Rome, people

content.cn_text: 认为最伟大的技能之一是能够发表令人印象深刻的演讲
content.en_text: thought that one of the greatest skills was the ability to give an impressive speech

content.cn_text: 公开演讲，而记忆力是做到这一点的关键之一。
content.en_text: in public, and memory was one of the keys to doing that.

content.cn_text: 所以这就是为什么从约公元前100年起，罗马人想要发展技巧来提高
content.en_text: So this was why, from about 100 BC, the Romans wanted to develop techniques to improve

content.cn_text: 记忆力。
content.en_text: memory.

content.cn_text: 这里一个重要人物是西塞罗，一位罗马政治领袖。
content.en_text: And an important figure here was Cicero, a Roman political leader.

content.cn_text: 西塞罗的观点之一是，人类实际上有两种记忆。
content.en_text: One of Cicero's ideas was that humans actually have two types of memory.

content.cn_text: 一种是可以被发展的记忆，他称之为人工记忆；还有
content.en_text: There's a memory that can be developed, which he called the artificial memory, and there's

content.cn_text: 另一种记忆，他称之为自然记忆。
content.en_text: another type of memory which he called natural.

content.cn_text: 关键是，你可以学习技巧来帮助提高人工记忆，这一
content.en_text: And the point is that you can learn techniques to help improve your artificial memory, an

content.cn_text: 观点从那以后一直让一些学者感兴趣。
content.en_text: idea that's interested some academics ever since.

content.cn_text: 接下来进入欧洲后期历史。首先要注意的是，在
content.en_text: So moving next to later European history, and the first thing to note is that, during

content.cn_text: 中世纪，一群被称为经院哲学家的学者也对
content.en_text: the Middle Ages, a group of academics called the Scholastics were also interested in

content.cn_text: 记忆技巧感兴趣。
content.en_text: memory techniques.

content.cn_text: 经院哲学家在向学生教授宗教时使用这些技巧，
content.en_text: The Scholastics used these techniques when they were instructing pupils about religion,

content.cn_text: 这提升了记忆技巧的重要性。
content.en_text: and this raised the profile of memory techniques.

content.cn_text: 但后来，人们对记忆的态度开始改变，这始于17世纪。
content.en_text: But later, attitudes towards memory began to change, and this started in the 17th century.

content.cn_text: 人们开始把记忆训练看作一种艺术形式，而这不再那么吸引他们，
content.en_text: People began to see memory training as an art form, and that didn't interest them so

content.cn_text: 因为他们认为科学能够回答大多数人类问题。
content.en_text: much anymore, because they thought that science had the answers to most human questions.

content.cn_text: 这种态度也一直延续到19世纪。
content.en_text: This attitude really continued into the 19th century as well.

content.cn_text: 孩子们不再被教授记忆技巧，而是被要求学习大量事实，
content.en_text: Children weren't taught memory techniques, but were expected to learn lots of facts,

content.cn_text: 并通过不断重复诸如日期之类的内容来做到这一点。
content.en_text: and to do this by constantly repeating things like dates and so on.

content.cn_text: 好，那么接下来我们来到现代。在20世纪40年代，一系列引人注目的研究
content.en_text: Okay, so then we come to modern times, and in the 1940s a remarkable series of studies was

content.cn_text: 围绕一位仅被称为S的男子展开。心理学家发现S从不记任何笔记，
content.en_text: conducted with a man known simply as S. Psychologists found that S never made notes about anything,

content.cn_text: 尽管他是一名记者，却从不忘记事实。
content.en_text: although he was a journalist and never forgot facts.

content.cn_text: 研究人员得出结论，S患有一种罕见的医学状况，这意味着他发展出了
content.en_text: The researchers concluded that S had an unusual medical condition, which meant he developed

content.cn_text: 惊人的记忆能力。这很重要，因为它似乎支持了古代
content.en_text: incredible memory skills. This was important as it seemed to support the ideas of the ancient

content.cn_text: 希腊人以及其他更早期学者的观点，但在20世纪后期，研究重点
content.en_text: Greeks and other much earlier scholars, but in the later part of the 20th century, the focus

content.cn_text: 转向了计算机。好了，这就是历史背景。现在我们继续。
content.en_text: switched to computers. Okay, well that's the historical context. Let's now turn.

groups.desc:
Complete the notes below.
Write ONE WORD ONLY for each answer.
groups.title: Questions 31-40.

items.title: Before 3000 BC, people were unable to 31. _______ so they relied on memory.
items.analysis: 答案解析：第31题问“people were unable to _______”，原文说 humans could not write，因此答案是 write。

items.title: People used memory to identify dangerous foods, such as some types of 32. _______.
items.analysis: 答案解析：第32题问危险食物的例子，原文说 this was particularly true of plants，因此答案是 plants。

items.title: Simonides believed that memories linked to a 33. _______ are easier to recall.
items.analysis: 答案解析：第33题问 memories linked to a _______ are easier to recall，原文说 connect it with one particular place，因此答案是 place。

items.title: In the 1st century, Greeks believed the senses, especially 34. _______, could help memory.
items.analysis: 答案解析：第34题问 senses, especially _______，原文说 particularly true when it came to sight，因此答案是 sight。

items.title: Making a good 35. _______ depended on memory.
items.analysis: 答案解析：第35题问 Making a good _______ depended on memory，原文说 give an impressive speech，因此答案是 speech。

items.title: From 100 BC, they developed techniques to improve memory. Cicero believed people have a memory that can be developed, in addition to their 36. _______ memory.
items.analysis: 答案解析：第36题问 in addition to their _______ memory，原文说 another type of memory which he called natural，因此答案是 natural。

items.title: Scholastics used earlier memory techniques to teach 37. _______.
items.analysis: 答案解析：第37题问 Scholastics used earlier memory techniques to teach _______，原文说 instructing pupils about religion，因此答案是 religion。

items.title: In the 17th century, people believed 38. _______ could provide explanations.
items.analysis: 答案解析：第38题问 people believed _______ could provide explanations，原文说 science had the answers，因此答案是 science。

items.title: In the 19th century, schoolchildren learned facts, such as 39. _______, using repetition.
items.analysis: 答案解析：第39题问 schoolchildren learned facts, such as _______，原文说 things like dates，因此答案是 dates。

items.title: In the 1940s, important research on “S”, who worked as a 40. _______, but people started to rely on computers.
items.analysis: 答案解析：第40题问 “S” worked as a _______，原文说 he was a journalist，因此答案是 journalist。

groups.source_collect:
Complete the notes below.
Write ONE WORD ONLY for each answer.
Human Memory
Early Cultures:
• Before 3000 BC, people were unable to $31$ so they relied on memory.
• Memory helped with navigation and family history.
• People used memory to identify dangerous foods, such as some types of $32$.
Ancient Greeks
• Simonides believed that memories linked to a $33$ are easier to recall.
• In the 1st century, Greeks believed the senses, especially $34$, could help memory.
Ancient Romans
• Making a good $35$ depended on memory.
• From 100 BC, they developed techniques to improve memory. Cicero believed people have a memory that can be developed, in addition to their $36$ memory.
Later European History
• Scholastics used earlier memory techniques to teach $37$.
• In the 17th century, people believed $38$ could provide explanations.
• In the 19th century, schoolchildren learned facts, such as $39$, using repetition.
Modern Times
• In the 1940s, important research on “S”, who worked as a $40$, but people started to rely on computers.
